question: Who works in India?

theme.label: Society, work, education, and services
readerQuestions: What basic capability is being measured?
How many people does the percentage represent?
Is this access, usage, quality, or outcome?
Which caveat stops the chart from being overclaimed?

plannedCharts.title: Whether you work at all comes down, first, to your sex
plannedCharts.subtitle: PLFS 2025 microdata · labour-force participation by sex
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Before education, caste or geography, the deepest fault line in who works in India is sex: nearly four in five men are in the workforce, but only two in five women.

plannedCharts.title: Most Indians don't have a job — they have work
plannedCharts.subtitle: PLFS 2025 microdata · workers by status in employment
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: The single most important fact about the shape of Indian work: the typical worker runs a tiny own-account enterprise or helps the family, rather than holding a salaried job.

plannedCharts.title: Nine in ten Indians work with no contract and no safety net
plannedCharts.subtitle: PLFS 2025 microdata · informal employment, overall and by location
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Informality is the water Indian workers swim in: no written contract, no social security, no paid leave — for the overwhelming majority.

plannedCharts.title: The cruel paradox: the more you study, the more likely you're jobless
plannedCharts.subtitle: PLFS 2025 microdata · unemployment rate by education level
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: This one chart dissolves the puzzle of low headline unemployment: joblessness is near zero for the unlettered and climbs steeply with every diploma.

plannedCharts.title: For the young, a degree is the riskiest qualification of all
plannedCharts.subtitle: PLFS 2025 microdata · youth unemployment by education level
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: The education paradox is sharpest at the start of working life, where India's graduate-unemployment crisis actually lives.

plannedCharts.title: Of the women who do work, almost a third are paid nothing
plannedCharts.unit: % of that sex's workers who are unpaid family labour
plannedCharts.subtitle: PLFS 2025 microdata · unpaid family workers, by sex
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Women's work is not only rarer, it is far more likely to be invisible — unpaid labour on the family farm or in the family shop.

plannedCharts.title: Marriage decides whether a woman works — necessity decides harder
plannedCharts.subtitle: PLFS 2025 microdata · female participation by marital status
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: A woman's place in the labour market shifts with her marital status in a way a man's never does.

plannedCharts.title: A woman's faith tracks whether she works
plannedCharts.subtitle: PLFS 2025 microdata · female participation by religion
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Religion is another axis along which the female labour market splits, with Muslim women's participation among the lowest.

plannedCharts.title: Same country, a completely different deal for women in every state
plannedCharts.subtitle: PLFS 2025 microdata · female labour-force participation (ps+ss, 15+) by state
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Where a woman is born changes her odds of working more than almost any national policy — the spread across the map is enormous.

plannedCharts.title: Who gets handed the hardest, least secure work
plannedCharts.unit: % of that group's workers in casual labour
plannedCharts.subtitle: PLFS 2025 microdata · casual-labour share by social group
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Caste still sorts Indians into kinds of work: the most insecure, day-to-day casual labour falls disproportionately on Dalits and Adivasis.

plannedCharts.title: ...and who gets the secure salaried jobs
plannedCharts.unit: % of that group's workers in regular salaried jobs
plannedCharts.subtitle: PLFS 2025 microdata · regular-salaried share by social group
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: The mirror image of the casual-work chart: the secure, salaried jobs concentrate at the top of the caste order.

plannedCharts.title: The caste pay gap
plannedCharts.subtitle: PLFS 2025 microdata · median monthly salaried earnings by social group
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Even among those who do hold a salaried job, what it pays still tracks caste.

plannedCharts.title: Why measured unemployment is lowest for the most disadvantaged
plannedCharts.subtitle: PLFS 2025 microdata · unemployment rate by social group
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Unemployment rates barely differ by caste — and that flatness is itself revealing.

plannedCharts.title: Unemployment in India is almost entirely a young person's condition
plannedCharts.subtitle: PLFS 2025 microdata · unemployment rate by age band
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: The headline jobless rate of under 4% hides the fact that unemployment is overwhelmingly concentrated among the young.

plannedCharts.title: The young women who vanish from the data
plannedCharts.unit: % of youth 15–29 not in employment, education or training
plannedCharts.subtitle: PLFS 2025 microdata · NEET rate by sex
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: The missing-women story has a sharp young edge: a huge share of young women are neither working, studying, nor training.

plannedCharts.title: In the village, you mostly work for yourself
plannedCharts.subtitle: PLFS 2025 microdata · rural workers by status
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: The rural and urban labour markets are almost different economies; in the countryside, self-employment and casual work dominate.

plannedCharts.title: In the city, you mostly work for a wage
plannedCharts.subtitle: PLFS 2025 microdata · urban workers by status
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Cities are where the salaried job lives — about half of urban workers have one, against one in seven in villages.

plannedCharts.title: What India's workers actually do all day
plannedCharts.subtitle: PLFS 2025 microdata · workers by broad industry
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: For all the talk of a services superpower, the largest single thing Indian workers do is farm.

plannedCharts.title: Even a salaried job often isn't a 'good' job
plannedCharts.subtitle: PLFS 2025 microdata · regular-salaried workers lacking each protection
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: The salaried job is the aspiration — but for half of those who have one, it comes without the protections that are supposed to define it.

plannedCharts.title: What a month of work pays — and who gets which deal
plannedCharts.subtitle: PLFS 2025 microdata · median monthly salaried earnings
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Pay is where every divide in this story compounds: formal vs informal, men vs women.

plannedCharts.title: The poorer the household, the more precarious the work
plannedCharts.subtitle: PLFS 2025 microdata · informality by household consumption quartile
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Informality and poverty reinforce each other: bad work keeps households poor, and poor households can only get bad work.

plannedCharts.title: Stack the advantages, and this is the gap
plannedCharts.subtitle: PLFS 2025 microdata · share with a regular salaried job + social security, by stacked identity
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Every divide in this story is a single cut; this is what happens when you stack them. It is the whole argument in one chart.

selectionRules: Start from the page question, not from the indicator name.
Choose the smallest set of visuals that explains scale, trend, structure, comparison, and caveat.
Prefer mobile-friendly charts: line charts with sparse ticks, horizontal bars, share strips, pyramids only when essential, and no dense tables.
Use one strong pull quote only when the evidence supports it with a number.
Add glossary blocks for technical terms that normal readers may not know.
caveats: Use only figures present in lockedNumbers or sourceSummaries.
If a related theme indicator is present, you may use it for context, but do not pretend it is the main answer.
If source coverage is thin, say so directly.
Do not compare states, districts, or cities unless the evidence packet contains those geographies.
forbiddenClaims: Do not infer causality from a trend unless the evidence explicitly supports it.
Do not cite a source that is not present in sourceSummaries.
